title: The Distressed Travellers; Or, Labour In Vain.
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A New Song, to a Tune never sung before.


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I sing of a journey to Clifton,[1]
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We would have performed, if we could;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Without cart or barrow, to lift on
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Poor Mary[2] and me through the mud.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Slee, sla, slud,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Stuck in the mud;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh it is pretty to wade through a flood!


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So away we went, slipping and sliding;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hop, hop, ן¿½ la mode de deux frogs;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tis near as good walking as riding,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When ladies are dressed in their clogs.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wheels, no doubt,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Go briskly about,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But they clatter and rattle, and make such a rout.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ dialogue
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ she.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Well! now, I protest it is charming;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How finely the weather improves!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That cloud, though ן¿½tis rather alarming,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How slowly and stately it moves.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ he.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Pshaw! never mind,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tis not in the wind,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We are travelling south, and shall leave it behind.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ she.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I am glad we are come for an airing,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For folks may be pounded, and pennן¿½d,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Until they grow rusty, not caring
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To stir half a mile to an end.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ he.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The longer we stay,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The longer we may;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Itן¿½s a folly to think about weather or way.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ she.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But now I begin to be frighted,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If I fall, what a way I should roll!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I am glad that the bridge was indicted,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Stay! stop! I am sunk in a hole!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ he.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Nay never care,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tis a common affair;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Youן¿½ll not be the last that will set a foot there.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ she.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Let me breathe now a little, and ponder
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On what it were better to do;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That terrible lane I see yonder,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I think we shall never get through.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ he.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½So think I:ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But, by the bye,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We never shall know, if we never should try.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ she.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But should we get there, how shall we get home
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What a terrible deal of bad road we have past!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Slipping, and sliding, and if we should come
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To a difficult stile, I am ruinן¿½d at last!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh this lane!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now it is plain
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That struggling and striving is labour in vain.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ he.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Stick fast there while I go and look;ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ she.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Donן¿½t go away, for fear I should fall:ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ he.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I have examined it, every nook,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And what you see here is a sample of all.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Come, wheel round,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The dirt we have found
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Would be an estate, at a farthing a pound.ן¿½


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now, sister Anne,[3] the guitar you must take,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Set it, and sing it, and make it a song:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I have varied the verse, for varietyן¿½s sake,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And cut it off shortן¿½because it was long.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tis hobbling and lame,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which critics wonן¿½t blame,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For the sense and the sound, they say, should be the same.
meaning:
"The Distressed Travellers; Or, Labour In Vain." is a quintessential example of William Cowper's signature style...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
